worth_it_explanation: First-party data is the audience signal Google Ads keeps as third-party cookies disappear and modeled targeting takes over more of the work. An account with no Customer Match list uploaded, or with a list that Google rejects because the data terms were never accepted, has nothing of its own to remarket to or to seed similar audiences from. The checks here are inspection-only. The 'Audiences' section and Data Manager in the account show whether a list exists, whether it is matching, and whether the customer data terms are accepted, so an auditor can read the gap without touching the advertiser's data.
intro: First-party data is the customer information an advertiser collected directly, such as an email address from a checkout or a form, and uploaded into Google Ads as a Customer Match audience. As browsers restrict third-party cookies, this data is what an account uses to re-engage existing customers and to seed similar audiences. These checks confirm that at least one Customer Match list exists and is matching, that the account accepted the customer data terms the feature depends on, and that the connections in Google Ads Data Manager are actually feeding data rather than sitting idle.

audit_checks.title: No Customer Match audiences are uploaded to the account
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a Customer Match list, the account cannot remarket to its own customers or seed similar audiences from them, so it relies on Google's modeled targeting alone. As third-party cookies erode, the absence of a first-party audience narrows what the account can target and removes a signal that bidding and prospecting both draw on.

educate.base:
Customer Match is the Google Ads feature that takes customer data an advertiser already holds, such as an email address collected at checkout, and turns it into an audience the account can target or exclude. The advertiser uploads a list of hashed customer records, Google matches those records against signed-in Google accounts, and the matched portion becomes a usable audience across Search, Shopping, YouTube, Gmail, and Display.

An account with no Customer Match list uploaded has no first-party audience of its own. It can still target Google's in-market and affinity segments, but it cannot remarket to its existing customers by their own data, and it cannot use those customers as the seed for a similar audience. As browsers remove third-party cookies, that first-party list is the part of the targeting an account controls directly rather than renting from Google's modeling.

A list also has to clear two bars before it does anything. The match rate has to be high enough that Google will serve to the audience, and the list cannot be stale. Customer Match memberships expire after 540 days without a refresh, so a list uploaded once and never updated shrinks as records age out.

A few signs point to a thin or absent first-party foundation:
  • The 'Audiences' section shows no 'Your data' segment built from a customer list
  • A list exists but reports a match rate too low to serve, or a member count below the minimum Google requires
  • A list was uploaded once and has not been refreshed, so its membership is aging out
You may want to confirm not only that a list exists but that it is matching and current. A stale or unmatched list is close to having none at all.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store usually has the richest source of first-party data since every purchase produces a consented email and an order history. You may want to confirm the account uploads a customer list from that purchase data and refreshes it on a schedule, rather than relying on a one-time export that ages out. A current purchaser list also seeds a strong similar audience for prospecting.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation account collects emails through forms rather than purchases, so its first-party list is built from inquiries and closed deals. You may want to confirm those captured emails reach Google Ads as a Customer Match list, and consider segmenting by stage, for example separating raw leads from closed customers, so the audience matches the action the campaign is optimizing toward.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the account has at least one Customer Match list, and whether that list is matching and current rather than empty or stale.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Audiences' section under 'Audiences, keywords, and content', and look at the 'Your data segments' list.
Check whether any segment is a customer list rather than a website or app segment. If none is, the account has no Customer Match audience uploaded.

For any customer list that does exist, open it and note the reported match rate and the member count. A list that reports too few matched members to serve, or that has not been refreshed in many months, is matching poorly even though it is present.

google_ads_data_manager.label: Google Ads Data Manager
steps: Open the 'Tools' menu and select 'Data manager'.
Review the connected data sources and their destinations, and note whether any connection sends data to Customer Match.
If no connection feeds Customer Match and no list was found in 'Audiences', the account has no first-party audience in place.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether at least one Customer Match list exists, and for any that exists, the match rate and the last refresh date. An account with a current list that matches well enough to serve passes. An account with no customer list, or with only a list that reports too low a match rate to serve or that has aged past a reasonable refresh window, is a fail recorded with what was found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a Customer Match list exists and, for any that does, its match rate and last refresh date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has no usable Customer Match audience in Google Ads, so the account cannot remarket to its own customers by their data or seed a similar audience from them. As browsers remove third-party cookies, this first-party list is the targeting signal the account controls directly, and its absence leaves the account leaning on modeled segments alone. {{voice_recommend}} uploading a customer list built from consented first-party data, such as purchaser or lead emails, and refreshing it on a schedule so its membership stays current. After the list is live, confirm in the 'Audiences' section that it reports a match rate high enough to serve.

audit_checks.title: Customer data terms are not accepted, so uploaded first-party data cannot match
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An account that uploads customer data without accepting the customer data terms, or without passing the consent signals Google requires for users in the European Economic Area (EEA), has its data rejected or held from matching. The list appears to exist while serving to far fewer users than its size implies, so the first-party audience the account is paying attention to does almost no work.

educate.base:
Uploading customer data into Google Ads is gated by policy as well as by setup. Before a Customer Match list matches and serves, the account has to accept Google's customer data terms, and the data itself has to be first-party information the advertiser collected directly, such as an email a customer entered on the site. Data bought from a third party, or scraped, is not permitted under the Customer Match policy.

There is a second gate for users in the EEA. To keep serving Customer Match to those EEA users, the advertiser has to pass consent signals alongside the uploaded data, indicating that the customer consented to this use. Without those signals, Google holds the EEA portion of the list back, so a list can look healthy in total while matching almost no one in Europe.

The effect of a missed term or a missing consent signal is quiet. The list still uploads, the account still shows an audience, but the matched and servable share is a fraction of what the member count suggests. The audience reads as present and does almost nothing.

A few conditions leave a first-party list unable to match:
  • The customer data terms were never accepted in the account, so uploads are rejected or withheld
  • The uploaded data was not collected in a first-party context, which the Customer Match policy does not allow
  • EEA consent signals are not passed, so the European share of the list is held from serving
You may want to confirm both the policy acceptance and the consent handling before treating an existing list as working. A list can pass the upload step and still fail to match for either reason.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store that ships to or advertises in Europe needs the consent signals in place for the EEA portion of its purchaser list, or that share is held from matching. You may want to confirm the consent the checkout collects is captured in a form the upload can pass to Google, rather than recorded only in the store's own records where Google never sees it.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation account often collects emails through forms that may not yet ask for the advertising consent Google requires for EEA users. You may want to confirm the form captures that consent where it applies, and that the captured value reaches the Customer Match upload, so European leads are eligible to match rather than silently withheld.

investigate.intro: Confirm that the account accepted the customer data terms, that the uploaded data is first-party, and that EEA consent signals are passed where they apply.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the customer list under the 'Audiences' section and read its status. A status that reports the list as not matching, or as awaiting accepted terms, points to a policy gate rather than a data problem.
Look for any message on the list or in account notifications about the customer data terms not being accepted, and note whether acceptance is outstanding.
Compare the list's total member count against the matched and servable count. A large gap, especially for an account serving Europe, points to data held back for a missing consent signal.

google_ads_data_manager.label: Google Ads Data Manager
steps: Open the 'Tools' menu, select 'Data manager', and open the connection that feeds Customer Match.
Check the connection's consent and terms settings, and note whether consent fields are mapped from the source data.
Record whether the connection reports any policy or consent message that would hold its data from matching.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the customer data terms are accepted, whether the uploaded data is first-party, and whether EEA consent signals are passed for accounts that serve Europe. An account with terms accepted, first-party data, and consent handled where it applies passes. An account with terms outstanding, with non-first-party data, or serving the EEA without consent signals is a fail recorded with the specific gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the customer data terms status, the data source, and the EEA consent handling above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} uploads first-party data into Google Ads without the policy acceptance and consent handling the data depends on, so the Customer Match list matches far fewer users than its size suggests. A list whose customer data terms are outstanding, or whose European members lack the required consent signals, is held back from serving while still appearing as an audience in the account. {{voice_recommend}} accepting the customer data terms in the account, confirming the uploaded data was collected in a first-party context, and passing consent signals for users in the European Economic Area where they apply. After the change, confirm the list's matched and servable count rises toward its total membership.

audit_checks.title: Google Ads Data Manager connections are unconfigured or idle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When first-party data lives in a warehouse or a customer relationship management (CRM) system but no Data Manager connection feeds it into Google Ads, the account relies on manual file uploads that lag and decay. The audience exists in the business but not in the account, so the first-party foundation is weaker and staler than the underlying data would allow.

educate.base:
Google Ads Data Manager is the point-and-click tool that connects an advertiser's first-party data sources to Google Ads, then sends that data to destinations such as Customer Match and offline conversion import. Instead of exporting a file and uploading it by hand each time, an advertiser connects a source once, for example BigQuery or a supported CRM platform, and the connection keeps the data flowing.

When no connection is configured, or a connection exists but sits idle, the first-party data in the business never reaches the account on a schedule. Someone has to remember to export and upload, the list ages between uploads, and a manual cadence is easy to let slip. The audience is as fresh as the last manual touch rather than as fresh as the source data.

An idle or absent connection rarely shows as an error. The account still runs, and any list that was uploaded manually still serves until it decays. The cost is the difference between a list that refreshes on its own and one that depends on a person to remember it.

A few conditions point to an underused first-party setup:
  • Data Manager shows no connected source feeding Customer Match or conversions
  • A connection exists but its last successful run is old, so data has stopped flowing
  • First-party data lives in a warehouse or CRM the team confirms is not connected to Google Ads
You may want to weigh whether a recurring connection would replace a manual upload that is slipping. A connection that refreshes on its own keeps the first-party audience current without depending on memory.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store's purchase data usually sits in a warehouse or a commerce platform that Data Manager can connect to, so the purchaser list can refresh automatically as new orders arrive. You may want to confirm that connection exists rather than relying on a periodic manual export, which leaves the most recent buyers out of the audience until the next upload.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation account's first-party data usually lives in a CRM system that tracks inquiries and deal stages. You may want to confirm a Data Manager connection feeds that CRM data into Google Ads on a schedule, so new and converted leads enter the audience without a manual export each cycle.

investigate.intro: Confirm whether Data Manager has a live connection feeding first-party data into Google Ads, or whether the account depends on manual uploads.

google_ads_data_manager.label: Google Ads Data Manager
steps: Open the 'Tools' menu and select 'Data manager'.
Review the connected data sources and note each one's destination, for example Customer Match or offline conversion import.

For each connection, note the last successful run. A connection with no recent run has stopped feeding data even though it still appears in the list. If no connection feeds Customer Match at all, the account depends on manual uploads.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the customer list under the 'Audiences' section and note its last update date.

Compare that date against how often the team says the underlying data changes. A list whose last update is far older than the data's natural refresh points to a manual cadence that is slipping rather than a live connection.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether Data Manager has a connection feeding Customer Match or conversions, the last successful run of any such connection, and where the account's first-party data actually lives. An account with a live, recently run connection feeding its first-party data passes. An account with no connection, or with a connection whose last run is stale while fresh data sits in a warehouse or CRM, is a fail recorded with the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Data Manager connection status, its last successful run, and where the account's first-party data lives above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is not feeding its first-party data into Google Ads through a Data Manager connection, so the account depends on manual uploads that lag and decay. The customer data exists in the business, in a warehouse or a customer relationship management (CRM) system, but it reaches the account only when someone remembers to export and upload it, which leaves the audience staler than the source data. {{voice_recommend}} connecting the first-party source through Data Manager so the data refreshes on a schedule rather than by hand. After the connection runs, confirm in Data Manager that its last successful run is recent and that it is sending data to the intended destination.
